sentences.text: Aron Bell.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A-R-O-N B-E-L-L.
First is the same, Aron.
The second was Bielsky, B-I-E-L-S-K-Y.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I see this is gonna be pretty hard on me.
I can see it right now.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was born near a village which had called Stankiewicze.
The closest town was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 15 kilometers away, which the name was Nowogrodek.
State of White Russia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which-- which it is now-- they call it Belarus.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In their language, the White Russian language, they call it Belarusia.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] January, 1930.
How old does it make me now?
What are we now, in '96?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 67.
David.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Same place where we were all born-- four generations.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] According to my older brothers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three or four generations in Stankiewicze.
He had a mill [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the villagers would come with grain and you make all kind of cereals and flour.
And we had a farm.
Whatever a farm has, that's what we had, whether it's a turkey or chicken or duck or a goose, horses, cows-- regular farm.
Some for our own consumption, and some they used to sell.
Bella.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] [SOBS] Her maiden name was Mendelewicz.
A village-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] [CRYING] a village called Stankiewicze.
I mean, [?
Petrovich, ?] forgive me, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like 10 kilometers away.
There were 10 brothers and two sisters-- 12.
Well, I'm not going to go through all of them.
It's really hard, as I said before, to relive the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING] it's very hard for me, anyway, as I said, to relive that.
I'll tell you some of them.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING] As I said, I come from a farm.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We were all born and brought up there.
I was the youngest out of 12.
By the time I was born, the oldest was already in the United States.
His name was Walter Bell.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Compared to other Jewish farmers, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the tough Jews in the area.

What I mean by that is I never experienced, as far as I can remember from six, seven years of age-- I never experienced any antisemitism in any way or manner, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because most of my brothers were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six foot over tall, and they were tough men.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] If any-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] if any the neighbors or anybody who ever knew them would never start up with the Bielsky family.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SIGHS] We're living a normal farmer's life.
My parents and some of my brothers that remained in the house [INAUDIBLE] were Asael and Zisl.
They worked on the farm or worked in the mill in order to support a family.
Well, one of my brothers was taken by my uncle.
He was ultra-religious, Orthodox.
So he wanted that at least one should grow up a Orthodox-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he should be Orthodox, should practice religion.
His name was Joshua Bielsky, and he became later on a rabbi.
At 22, he got [NON-ENGLISH].
Nowogrodek.

[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] We were living like-- I guess it's known to the world-- like farmers live [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up until the 22nd of June, 1941, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when the Nazis occupied the whole area.
Be my guest.
Well, there was an older-- Walter was already-- I'm-- what I am giving you the information is what I remember.
At those days, Tuvia was somewhere else, like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 300 kilometers away from our farm.
He was married and living a-- as a merchant.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I had another brother, Nathan, which was in America later on.
He went in the very low '30s, came to America.
A good question, which I don't know if I could answer.
I-- I asked later on, why didn't my parents leave?
So the answer was it was very hard to get a visa to the United States, and financial hardship.
It wasn't so easy to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] leave to another country.
But after those two fellows-- Walter and Nathan, who had an uncle here in the United States, and he sent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a girl to pick them up, in other words, one at a time.
And this is the way those two brothers were lucky enough to get to the United States.
I beg your pardon?
Yeah, got married there, and this is the way-- later on-- that was before my time.
Well, I was born in 1930, and the oldest was born in 1904.
So I should say so.
And the other one, Nathan, was born in 1913.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] At home lived another one, Abraham, Yankel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Asael.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They were before my time.
You have to understand that.
One is still alive, in Florida.
Her name is Estelle Eshke.
The other one, Toby, passed away about a year and a half ago.
Estelle was home, and Toby was married to another Jewish farmer, 10 kilometers away.
Well, we had some help.
We had a woman that lived all her life with us and then a-- a kid, Gentile kid, grew up with us, and he worked on the farm.
Maria.
Maria was her name.
His name was Adolph Stishok.
Well, I don't remember him.
I do not remember him as a kid.
I remember him as a grown man.
I would say he probably was in the 30s when I remember him, in the 30s.
For my time, he took care of the mill, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mainly.
He lived with us.
My friends personally?
At 10 years old-- the neighbors kids.
But mainly, I guess, from Nowogrodek, when I went to school for three years.
Regular grammar school.
Public school.
Mixed.
Mixed, but mainly Jewish.
Yes, most of them.
Well, I never-- maybe I was lucky.
I never experienced any antisemitism in any way or manner.
Well, we had a large extended family, of course.
We had uncles, cousins.
But none of us lived with us.
They only used to come to visit us on vacation time, July and August.
Population was about 10 families.
Obvious they were Gentile.
All White Russian except one family was Polish.
No.
The closest Jewish family was, like, 10 kilometers, 12 kilometers away from us.
Yiddish and White Russian.
My father and mother were religious.
They would not travel on the Sabbath.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They ate kosher.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were traditional [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jews, except, as I mentioned before, one grew up an ultra Orthodox rabbi, which he died about 10 years ago.
Any holiday was nice.
Well, this was every Friday night.
They would put a white-- I don't even know how to say it in English-- a white cloth to cover the table.
And-- and [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] my mother [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] would light candles.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think we always had a salad or a holiday meat.
It was somewhat different than the regular day.
Well, I would go swimming.
I would go fishing.
I would see how people work on the farm, how people work at the mill.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was my pastime.
Not until the war-- well, no, actually, not until the war started.
A regular farmer's big family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little house, it would call in our terms, in today's terms.
We had one living room and one bedroom and a big kitchen.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We were not rich, obvious, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but we always had enough to live on.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only different from our house on the farm would be-- and a Gentile family would be that it's-- the table would be covered and maybe a challah would lay on the white cloth, and candles would be lit.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You mean you want the names of neighbors?
Oh, I remember all of them well.
Well, one of the neighbors saved my life.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Two brothers and a sister.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Their name was-- the younger one was Kostas, the other one was Mikhas, and their sister was Anzhia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] None of them are alive.
Only one of those brother's wife is alive, which I visited them a couple of years back.
Those are the people that actually saved my life, in a way.
Well, I remember all of them.
I remember the good and the bad.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So they were actually very good.
They were the best, I guess, of the neighbors.
But all of our neighbors were good people, except one, the Polish family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess, as far as I'm concerned, they were bad.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Their name was Kuszel.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the war started, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody was home except me and my parents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My two brothers-- those main two brothers that I will talk about are Asael and Zisl.
They were in the Russian army.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So I was actually the only one, as far as the kids go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Abraham and Yankel-- after a week or so, they came also home from the city.
After a week of what?
I'm sorry.
After a week-- in a week after the war started.
This is what I'm telling you now.
We were they only one in the village that actually did have a radio, correct.
And I remember people from all around the area, like 20 kilometers away, would come and listen to the radio.
I think this was-- when we got the-- the radio-- I don't recall, really, whatever-- it was '39 or '40 or '41, one of those three years.
Not in '41-- earlier. '39 or '40.
Oh, yes, talking politics with neighbors.
Used to come, neighbors, and say to my father that he should take his family and escape, because a bad time is coming up in the state of White Russia.
We knew.
But I don't think most of the population believed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those people.
One of my brothers, Abraham-- he married to one of those girls that escaped from deep Poland, where it was occupied by the Nazis, I guess, in 1939.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was telling us stories.
But then behind her back, I remember my mother, my father would not believe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the Nazis just random killing Jews.
And we were not the only ones who did not believe.
Others also-- other Jewish people-- did not trust or believe those few Jews that escaped from the Nazi-occupied Poland into state of White Russia.
I hope I've made myself clear.
Yes, they spoke about the Nazis, of course.
Me as a child?
Not too much, really, but I wouldn't-- I couldn't answer that.
I couldn't answer that.
Well, the war started the 22nd of June, 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And within two or three days, really, the Nazis dropped [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- what do you call?
The parachute people, parachute soldiers, and the Nazis were even in our village.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is where-- from here I can tell you, for the next few years, our problem.
When did I first see them?
I saw them, as I said, two or three days right after the war started.
Nazi soldiers coming with one Gentile policeman.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Were coming to us, looking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for two brothers, Asael and Zisl.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was the very first time when I would see, personally, these soldiers or the policemen would come, I would run away and hide myself-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and hide myself.
They were starting to come every day, and that's where the terror started in our house.
It was hard.
It is very hard.
I don't think I'm intelligent enough to bring it across to you, the terror that we went through at the time.
